1:
"Then said he unto the disciples, It is impossible but that offences will come: but woe unto him, through whom they come!" The witnesses note the certainty of offences and the woe upon their authors. Theophylact sets the occasion: having rebuked the covetous Pharisees, Christ "declares them to be men who caused division, and placed obstacles in the divine way... It is impossible but that offences will come, that is, hindrances to a good life which is pleasing to God." Cyril distinguishes two kinds of offence: "the one resist the glory of God" — heresies and words against the truth — "but the other serve only to cause a stumbling-block to the brethren, as strifes, slanders, and the like"; and he judges the latter chiefly in view here, since Christ adds, "If thy brother trespass against thee, rebuke him."

The witnesses note the two truths held together: offences are inevitable in a fallen world, yet the inevitability excuses no man — "woe unto him through whom they come."

So the witnesses make the verse a solemn warning against being a cause of stumbling. The lesson the expositors draw is that the certainty of offences neither excuses those who give them nor permits the disciple to give them lightly. Christ, the witnesses note, declares it "impossible but that offences will come" — in a world of sin and weakness, occasions of stumbling, hindrances to godly living, temptations that draw men from the good way, must inevitably arise; the disciple is not to be surprised when they do. Yet, the expositors press, this inevitability is no shelter for the offender: "woe unto him through whom they come" — the man who causes another to stumble, who places an obstacle in the way of a brother's faith or holiness, brings a fearful woe upon himself, however common such offences are. The witnesses note Cyril's useful distinction between offences that assail the glory of God (false doctrine, heresy) and those that wound the brethren (strife, slander, bad example); and they observe that the context, with its call to rebuke and forgive, points chiefly to the latter — the personal offences between brother and brother. The expositors draw the searching application: each disciple must take heed that he be not the channel of stumbling to another, neither by false teaching nor by sin that wounds or misleads; for though offences will come, it were better they came through any other than through him. The verse thus opens a section on the duties of the Christian community — to avoid giving offence, to rebuke and forgive the offending brother — by pressing the grave responsibility that lies on every cause of stumbling, that the disciple may walk warily, fearing to be the one through whom the inevitable woe is brought.
2:
"It were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he cast into the sea, than that he should offend one of these little ones." The witnesses note the severity of the warning and the proverbial image. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot and Clarke explain the "millstone" as the heavy stone of an ass-mill (turned by a beast), distinguished from the lighter hand-mill, and note that "to have a millstone hanged about the neck was a common proverb" for an intolerable burden, while drowning with a stone about the neck "was an ancient mode of punishment." Clarke cites the rabbinic saying of one who would study the law "with a millstone about his neck" — a burden that makes the task impossible.

The witnesses note the comparison: it were "better" — a less dreadful fate — for the offender to be drowned in the sea than to bring the guilt of offending one of Christ's little ones.

So the witnesses make the verse the measure of the guilt of giving offence. The lesson the expositors draw is the fearful seriousness with which Christ regards the wounding of the weak in faith. The image, the witnesses note, is one of utter ruin: a heavy millstone, such as a donkey turns, hanged about the neck, and the man cast into the depths of the sea — a death sudden, certain, and without escape. And Christ declares this dreadful end "better" for the offender than the alternative — better to suffer such a drowning than to "offend one of these little ones." The expositors press the force of the comparison: temporal death, however terrible, is light beside the guilt and the doom of causing a believer to stumble; the one ends the body's life, but the other imperils the soul, both of the offended and the offender. The "little ones," the witnesses observe, are not children only but the weak in faith, the humble believers, the new and tender disciples whom Christ holds especially dear and guards with special jealousy — so that to despise, mislead, or wound one of them is to incur his particular displeasure. The expositors draw the abiding warning: those who, by false teaching, bad example, harshness, or contempt, cause the weak to stumble in their faith or fall into sin, bring upon themselves a guilt heavier than the worst of deaths; and the disciple is to handle the weak with the utmost tenderness, fearing above all to be a stone of stumbling to them. The verse thus presses the lesson of the first with overwhelming weight: the offending of one little one is a crime so grave that drowning were the lighter fate — a measure of how precious the weak are to Christ, and how jealously he will avenge their hurt.
3:
"Take heed to yourselves: If thy brother trespass against thee, rebuke him; and if he repent, forgive him." The witnesses note the duty of faithful rebuke joined to ready forgiveness. Ambrose sets it after the warning of judgment: "Christ added a commandment to give forgiveness to those who turn themselves from their trespasses, lest any one through despair should not be reclaimed." Theophylact draws out the balance: "that there might neither be hard-wrung pardon, nor a too easy forgiveness, neither a harsh upbraiding to dishearten, nor an overlooking of faults to invite to sin" — for "better is a friendly correction than a quarrelsome accusation; the one strikes shame into a man, the other moves his indignation. He who is admonished will more likely be saved, because he fears to be destroyed."

The witnesses note the twofold duty: not to ignore a brother's sin (which would harm him), but to rebuke it faithfully; and, on his repentance, to forgive freely.

So the witnesses make the verse the rule of brotherly correction and forgiveness. The lesson the expositors draw is the union of faithfulness and mercy in dealing with an offending brother. The disciple, the witnesses note, is first warned, "Take heed to yourselves" — to guard his own spirit in the matter, lest in correcting another he sin himself. Then comes the twofold duty. First, faithful rebuke: "if thy brother trespass against thee, rebuke him" — not to nurse the grievance in silence, nor to publish it abroad, but (as Theophylact notes from the parallel, Matthew 18:15) to tell him his fault privately, in love, seeking his recovery. The expositors press the wisdom of this: a friendly, private correction shames a man into repentance, where a public or quarrelsome accusation only provokes his indignation and hardens him; love seeks not to expose but to reclaim. Second, ready forgiveness: "if he repent, forgive him" — the rebuke aiming not at punishment but at restoration, so that the moment the brother turns, forgiveness must follow. The witnesses observe Theophylact's careful balance: the disciple is to avoid both extremes — neither a harsh upbraiding that drives the offender to despair, nor an easy overlooking of sin that emboldens him to continue; neither a grudging pardon, nor a forgiveness so cheap it makes light of the fault. Ambrose's note, the expositors observe, grounds the whole in mercy: the command to forgive the penitent is given lest any sinner, despairing of pardon, be lost. The verse thus sets the Christian rule for offences within the brotherhood: faithful, loving rebuke of the sin, and free, ready forgiveness of the repentant — the discipline of love that seeks always the brother's restoration, not his ruin.
4:
"And if he trespass against thee seven times in a day, and seven times in a day turn again to thee, saying, I repent; thou shalt forgive him." The witnesses note the unlimited measure of forgiveness. The Reformed Gill reads the condition of true repentance: forgive him "as often as he sins, and appears to be truly sensible of it... not only barely in words professes his concern, but there is reason to believe he is heartily grieved, and to hope he will behave better." The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the text and harmonizes with Matthew: "the better MSS. omit 'against thee,' making the command more general," and notes the teaching "is here manifestly reproduced" from Matthew 18:21-22 — though the form is "seven times" rather than "seventy times seven," "the meaning is obviously the same; no accumulation of offences, however often repeated, is" to exhaust forgiveness, since "seven times a day" presses the same boundless mercy.

The witnesses note the staggering measure: seven offences in a single day, each followed by repentance, all to be forgiven — a forgiveness without limit.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of unlimited forgiveness toward the penitent brother. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disciple's forgiveness, like God's, is to know no limit where there is repentance. The number "seven times in a day," the witnesses note, is not a ceiling but a figure of completeness, pressing the same boundless mercy as Matthew's "seventy times seven" — Christ means that no number of repeated offences, however often they recur, may exhaust the disciple's readiness to forgive. The expositors press the condition that accompanies it: each offence is followed by the brother's turning again, "saying, I repent"; the forgiveness answers to repentance, and Gill notes the repentance must be sincere — not a hollow word, but a real grief and a credible turning. Yet, the witnesses observe, the disciple is not to play the suspicious judge of his brother's heart, refusing pardon on doubt of his sincerity; where the brother professes repentance, forgiveness is to be granted, the matter of his sincerity left to God. The expositors draw the searching lesson: human nature wearies of forgiving, and counts a few pardons enough; but Christ requires a forgiveness as inexhaustible as the offences are repeated, modelled on the mercy of God, who pardons his children times without number. Ellicott's harmonizing note, the witnesses observe, shows that Christ taught this truth more than once, in different forms, its substance always the same: that the wronged disciple, so often as the offender repents, must forgive. The verse thus sets the measure of Christian forgiveness at no measure at all — a mercy that keeps no count, answering every repentance with pardon, as the believer himself is daily and freely forgiven of God.
5:
"And the apostles said unto the Lord, Increase our faith." The witnesses note the disciples' request and its occasion. Theophylact ties it to the hard duties just enjoined: "the disciples hearing our Lord discoursing of certain arduous duties, such as poverty and avoiding offences, entreat Him to increase their faith, that they might be able to follow poverty (for nothing so prompts to a life of poverty as faith and hope in the Lord) and through faith to guard against giving offences." Gregory reads it as a prayer for growth: "that the faith already received in its beginning might go on increasing more and more to perfection." Lightfoot offers a distinctive suggestion: that the apostles asked Christ to "lay down something concerning the measure of our faith, as thou hast done concerning the measure of our charity" — to define faith's measure as he had defined forgiveness.

The witnesses note the apostles, feeling the weight of the duties Christ laid on them, turn not to their own resolve but to him for an increase of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' prayer for stronger faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that the demands of discipleship drive the believer to seek a greater faith from Christ. The apostles, the witnesses note, had just heard hard sayings — to forgive without limit, to avoid giving offence, to embrace poverty — duties beyond their natural strength; and feeling their insufficiency, they make the right response: not "give us greater resolve" or "make the demands lighter," but "Increase our faith." The expositors press the wisdom of this: faith is the root of obedience, the spring of all the graces; a man forgives, bears, and renounces by faith and hope in God, and where faith is weak these duties prove impossible. Theophylact's observation, the witnesses observe, is apt — nothing so enables a life of self-denial and patience as a strong faith in the Lord; so the apostles, rightly sensing their need, ask for the very thing that would make the hard duties possible. Gregory's reading, the expositors note, marks the nature of the request: not faith newly given, but the faith already begun increased and perfected — a prayer every believer may make, who feels his faith too small for the demands upon it. The witnesses note that Christ does not rebuke the request but answers it, both by showing (in the next verse) the great power of even a little true faith, and by pressing (in the parable that follows) the humility that faith should breed. The verse thus models the believer's response to the high calling of Christ: conscious of weakness, he turns to the Lord for an increase of faith — the grace by which alone the impossible duties of discipleship are made possible.
6:
"And the Lord said, If ye had faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye might say unto this sycamine tree, Be thou plucked up by the root, and be thou planted in the sea; and it should obey you." The witnesses note the power of even the least true faith. The grammatical-historical Barnes identifies the "sycamine" as the mulberry tree (so the Vulgate and Syriac), distinct from the sycamore. Lightfoot notes "as a grain of mustard seed" was a common Jewish phrase for the smallest measure. The point, the witnesses agree, is the contrast between the smallness of the seed and the greatness of the effect: a faith as small as a mustard seed, if genuine, could command a deep-rooted tree to be planted in the sea and be obeyed.

The witnesses note Christ answers the request for more faith by showing how much even a little true faith can do — magnifying the power not of great faith but of real faith.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's answer to the prayer "Increase our faith." The lesson the expositors draw is the mighty power lodged in even the smallest grain of genuine faith. The mustard seed, the witnesses note, was proverbially the least of seeds; and the sycamine (mulberry) tree was deep-rooted, hard to uproot — so that the saying joins the smallest cause to the most improbable effect. Christ's meaning, the expositors press, is not that the disciples lacked faith entirely, but to show them, and to encourage their request, how much true faith can accomplish: were their faith but as a grain of mustard seed — small in measure but real in kind — it could do what seems impossible, commanding a deep-rooted tree to be torn up and planted in the sea, and being obeyed. The witnesses observe that the power lies not in the quantity of faith but in its genuineness and in the God on whom it lays hold: a little faith, truly resting on the omnipotence of God, can move what no human strength could stir. The expositors note the encouragement to the disciples: they need not despair of the hard duties for want of a great faith; let their faith be real, however small, and it will prove sufficient, drawing on the boundless power of God. The "planting in the sea," the witnesses observe, is a vivid hyperbole — a thing contrary to nature, impossible to man — marking that faith's effects are bounded only by the will and power of God, not by the measure of the believer. The verse thus answers the apostles' prayer by lifting their eyes from the size of their faith to its true nature and its almighty object: a mustard-seed faith, genuine and God-reliant, is enough for things that seem impossible — so let them seek not a great faith only, but a real one, and use the little they have.
7:
"But which of you, having a servant plowing or feeding cattle, will say unto him by and by, when he is come from the field, Go and sit down to meat?" The witnesses note the parable of the servant and its guard against pride. Theophylact explains its purpose: "because faith makes its possessor a keeper of God's commandments, and adorns him with wonderful works, it would seem a man might thereby fall into pride; our Lord therefore forewarned His Apostles by a fit example, not to boast themselves in their virtues." Augustine reads it of the present life of labour: "be the servant in the field ploughing or feeding, that is, in this life following his worldly" task — the labour that precedes the rest and reward.

The witnesses note the common sense of the picture: a master does not, when his servant comes in from the field, at once bid him sit and eat, but expects further service first.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the parable of the unprofitable servants. The lesson the expositors draw is the guard against spiritual pride that must accompany faith and obedience. Christ, the witnesses note, having shown the great power of faith, immediately checks the pride that such power might breed: lest the apostles, made strong in faith and fruitful in works, should boast themselves and think God their debtor, he sets before them the figure of the servant. The picture, the expositors press, is drawn from common life and the relations of master and slave: no master, when his servant comes in weary from ploughing or tending the flock, says at once, "Go and sit down to meat" — as though the servant had earned rest and the master owed him ease; rather, the master expects the servant to continue his service, preparing the master's meal, before he takes his own. The point, the witnesses observe (developed in the verses that follow), is that the servant, having done all, has only done his duty, and may claim no reward as of debt. Theophylact's note, the expositors observe, marks the danger the parable guards against: faith adorns a man with works, and works tempt him to pride — to imagine he has put God in his debt; against this Christ presses the servant's proper humility. Augustine's reading, the witnesses note, sets the labour in this present life, the rest and reward in the world to come. The verse thus begins to teach the disciple, however faithful and fruitful, to think of himself as a servant who owes all his service to his Master, and may demand nothing — preparing for the great lesson of humility that the parable will press: "we are unprofitable servants; we have done that which was our duty to do."
8:
"And will not rather say unto him, Make ready wherewith I may sup, and gird thyself, and serve me, till I have eaten and drunken; and afterward thou shalt eat and drink?" The witnesses note the master's natural expectation of continued service. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the construction expects the answer "yes" — the master will indeed require the servant to serve first. Ellicott draws a beautiful connection: "the words receive a fresh significance if we connect them with Luke 12:37," where the Master himself girds himself to serve his watching servants — "There the Master promises that He will gird Himself and minister to His disciples; here He tells them that He too requires a service." The witnesses note the deeper meat the disciples must give their Lord: "the meat and the drink of seeing that His Father's will is done on earth" (John 4:34); and then they too shall share his joy (Revelation 3:20).

The witnesses note the order the master sets: the servant serves first, and only afterward eats — service before rest, duty before reward.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart of the parable's picture. The lesson the expositors draw is the priority of service and the secondary place of the servant's own ease. The master, the witnesses note, does not invert the order of master and servant, bidding the weary labourer sit while he serves him; rather, he requires the servant, even after his day in the field, to "gird thyself, and serve me" — to prepare and wait at the master's table — and only "afterward" to eat and drink himself. This, the expositors press, is the proper relation of the servant to his lord: the master's interests come first, the servant's rest and refreshment after; the servant lives to serve, not to be served, and may not claim his ease until his master's will is done. Ellicott's connection to Luke 12:37, the witnesses observe, opens a deeper view: in that passage Christ promises that he himself, the great Master, will gird himself and serve his faithful, watching servants — a wonder of condescending grace; here he reminds them that he too has a claim upon their service, that they must minister to him before they rest. The two passages held together, the expositors note, teach both the disciple's duty to serve his Lord wholly, and the Lord's gracious promise at last to serve and reward his faithful servants — service now, rest and joy hereafter. The "meat" the disciples are to give their Master, the witnesses press (with Ellicott), is to do his Father's will, as Christ's own meat was to do the will of him that sent him (John 4:34); and having so served, they shall in the end share his table and his joy (Revelation 3:20). The verse thus sets the order of the servant's life: serve the Master first, claim rest and reward after — the humble priority of duty that the parable enforces, against all pride that would make God the servant's debtor.
9:
"Doth he thank that servant because he did the things that were commanded him? I trow not." The witnesses note the master's lack of obligation to the servant. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the form expects the answer "no" — "does he thank?" — the master owes no gratitude, for the servant only did his duty. Ellicott sets the saying in its place, guarding it from misuse: "the words are spoken from the standpoint of the old relations between master and slave, not from that of those who recognise that master and slave are alike children of the same Father"; their bearing is to check the self-righteousness "creeping in, even into the souls of the disciples, leading them to ask, What shall we have therefore?" (Matthew 19:27) and to seek high places.

The witnesses note the principle: the servant who does what he is commanded earns no special thanks, for he has rendered only what was owed.

So the witnesses make the verse the application of the parable to the disciple's claims. The lesson the expositors draw is that obedience to God's commands establishes no claim of merit, for it is but the rendering of what is due. The master, the witnesses note, "doth not thank that servant" — owes him no special gratitude, lays himself under no debt — "because he did the things that were commanded him"; the servant has done only his duty, and duty done earns no reward beyond itself. The expositors press the searching application: so it is with the disciple before God. However faithfully he serves, however much he obeys, he has only done what he was bound to do; he establishes no merit, puts God under no obligation, can demand no reward as of right. Ellicott's caution, the witnesses observe, guards the saying from a wrong inference: it speaks from the harsh standpoint of master and slave, not to deny that God graciously rewards his servants and calls them friends and children, but to crush the self-righteous spirit that would make God a debtor. And that spirit, the expositors note, was the very danger — for the disciples were already prone to ask "what shall we have therefore?" and to seek precedence, as though their service earned them a claim. Against this, the witnesses press, Christ sets the servant's humility: the believer is to serve God not for wages he can demand, nor to lay God under obligation, but as one who owes all his service and can claim nothing. The verse thus undercuts every thought of merit in obedience: the faithful servant earns no thanks of debt, for he has done only his duty — and the disciple, however diligent, must own that his best obedience gives him no claim upon God, but is itself the gift and the due of his Lord.
10:
"So likewise ye, when ye shall have done all those things which are commanded you, say, We are unprofitable servants: we have done that which was our duty to do." The witnesses note the great lesson of humility that crowns the parable. The Reformed Gill applies it to all Christ's servants, ministers and private believers alike, who "are as servants, and should be as laborious as the ploughman and the shepherd... continually employed; and when one work is done, another is to be taken in hand"; and "God and Christ are to be served by them in the first place, and then themselves." The witnesses note the confession Christ puts in their mouth: even having done all, they are to call themselves "unprofitable servants" who have done only their duty.

The witnesses note the striking word "unprofitable": not that their service is worthless to God, but that it adds nothing to him and earns them no claim — they have given only what was owed.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the lesson on humility. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disciple, even at his most faithful and fruitful, must disclaim all merit and own himself an unprofitable servant. Christ presses the application, the witnesses note: "when ye shall have done all those things which are commanded you" — supposing the disciple had rendered perfect and complete obedience, which none in fact attains — even then he is to say, "We are unprofitable servants; we have done that which was our duty to do." The expositors weigh the word "unprofitable": it does not mean the disciple's service is useless or unpleasing to God, for God graciously accepts and rewards it; but that it brings no profit or advantage to God, who is complete in himself and needs nothing, and that it lays no obligation upon him, for the servant has rendered only his due. The witnesses press the humility this enjoins: the believer is to serve God diligently and continually, taking up one work as another is finished, putting his Master's interest before his own; yet, having done all, he is to claim nothing, boast of nothing, and account himself an unprofitable servant who has merely discharged his duty. This confession, the expositors note, is the death-blow to all self-righteousness and merit-mongering: it forbids the disciple to think he has earned heaven, or put God in his debt, or deserved reward — for the best obedience is but the payment of a debt always owed. The witnesses observe the paradox of grace that lies behind it: though the servant can claim nothing, the gracious Master will yet reward him freely, as the wider Scripture shows; but the reward is of grace, not of debt. The verse thus seals the parable with the temper that must mark every servant of Christ: humble, diligent, continual service, joined to a deep disclaiming of all merit — "we are unprofitable servants" — that the disciple may serve much and boast nothing, resting his hope not on his own works but wholly on the grace of his Lord.
11:
"And it came to pass, as he went to Jerusalem, that he passed through the midst of Samaria and Galilee." The witnesses note the route and its symbolic fitness. Titus of Bostra reads the passage through both regions as a sign of Christ's reconciling work: "there was enmity between the Jews and the Samaritans, and He, to allay this, passed into the midst of both nations, that he might cement both into one new man." Cyril sets the coming miracle in the service of Christ's glory and the gathering of Israel and the nations alike to faith. The witnesses note that Christ, on his way to Jerusalem and the cross, passes through the border country where Jew and Samaritan met — the very place where the healing of the ten lepers, and the gratitude of the one Samaritan, would display the breadth of his grace.

The witnesses note the geography is not incidental: the route through Samaria and Galilee sets the stage for a miracle that crosses the deepest national divide.

So the witnesses make the verse the setting of the healing of the ten lepers. The lesson the expositors draw is the providential fitness of Christ's path through the borderland of Jew and Samaritan. The witnesses note that Christ, journeying toward Jerusalem and his passion, "passed through the midst of Samaria and Galilee" — the frontier where two peoples long divided by enmity touched; and the fathers find in this no accident but a sign. Titus of Bostra's reading, the expositors press, draws out the deeper meaning: the Saviour passes between the two nations to break down the wall of partition, to make of Jew and Gentile (here, Samaritan) "one new man" (Ephesians 2:14-15) — a reconciliation his cross would accomplish, and which the coming miracle foreshadows. For among the ten lepers, the witnesses observe, common misery had already broken the barrier — Jew and Samaritan together in their affliction — and Christ's grace would heal them all alike, while the one who returned to give thanks would be a Samaritan, a stranger to the covenant, putting the thankless Jews to shame. The expositors note the keynote of Luke's Gospel sounded here: the breadth of grace, reaching beyond Israel to the despised, gathering all peoples to faith. The verse thus sets the scene with quiet significance: Christ on the road to his cross, passing through the divided land, about to display in the healing of ten and the thanks of one the universal reach of his mercy and the reconciliation of the nations that his death would seal.
12:
"And as he entered into a certain village, there met him ten men that were lepers, which stood afar off." The witnesses note the lepers' condition and their standing at a distance. Cyril describes them as "banished from the towns and cities, and counted unclean according to the rites of the Mosaic law." Lightfoot explains how the ten came to be together despite the law that "he that is a leper shall dwell alone" (Leviticus 13:46): the rabbis held that lepers might consort with lepers, though "those under different defilements might not converse promiscuously" — so a company of lepers could band together, as the four at Samaria's gate (2 Kings 7:3). The witnesses note the lepers "stood afar off," kept at a distance by their uncleanness and the law that forbade their approach.

The witnesses note the company of ten — a fellowship of the afflicted, Jew and Samaritan together — kept apart from Christ by their disease, crying to him from a distance.

So the witnesses make the verse the meeting of Christ with the ten lepers. The lesson the expositors draw is the misery of the leper as an emblem of the sinner, and the barrier that sin, like leprosy, sets between the soul and God. The lepers, the witnesses note, were the outcasts of society: their disease, the type of sin in its loathsomeness and contagion, banished them from the city, cut them off from family and worship, and compelled them to "stand afar off," forbidden by the law to approach the clean. The expositors press the spiritual figure: as the leper was separated from his people and kept at a distance, so sin separates the sinner from God and from the fellowship of the holy; the soul defiled with sin's leprosy stands afar off, unable of itself to draw near. Lightfoot's note, the witnesses observe, explains the company: common affliction had drawn the ten together — even Jew and Samaritan, who would not otherwise mingle, were united by their shared misery, a fellowship of the wretched that the next verse shows crying with one voice. The expositors draw the lesson: misery levels the distinctions men prize; in the presence of a common ruin, the divisions of nation and party fall away, and the afflicted unite in their cry for help. The verse thus sets before us the ten lepers — outcast, unclean, standing afar off, yet drawn together and drawn toward Christ — a picture of sinners in their misery, kept at a distance by their defilement, yet able still to lift their voice to the one who can cleanse them; and it prepares for their united cry for mercy and the cleansing that Christ's word would work.
13:
"And they lifted up their voices, and said, Jesus, Master, have mercy on us." The witnesses note the lepers' united and earnest cry. Adam Clarke draws out the unity: "they cried with one accord — they were all equally necessitous, and there was but one voice among them all, though ten were engaged in crying at the same time; as they were companions in suffering, they were also companions in prayer." He presses the lesson: "Prayer should be strong and earnest when the disease is great and inveterate. Sin is the worst of all leprosies; it not only separates those to whom it cleaves from the righteous, but it separates them from God; and nothing but the pitying heart and powerful hand of Christ Jesus can set any soul free from it." Ellicott notes "Master" is Luke's usual word for "Rabbi," and that the lepers had likely heard of earlier cleansings (Matthew 8:2; 11:5).

The witnesses note the lepers cry from afar, with one voice, for mercy — owning Christ as Master and casting themselves on his pity.

So the witnesses make the verse the lepers' believing cry for mercy. The lesson the expositors draw is the pattern of earnest, united prayer in the consciousness of deep need. The lepers, the witnesses note, "lifted up their voices" — crying aloud, both because Christ was at a distance and because their need was urgent; and they cried "with one accord," Clarke observes, ten voices made one by a common misery, companions in suffering become companions in prayer. The expositors press the fitness of their cry: they call Christ "Master," owning his authority and likely his power to heal, of which they had heard; and they ask not for silver or bread but for "mercy" — casting themselves wholly on his compassion, claiming nothing, pleading only their misery. Clarke's application, the witnesses observe, is searching: prayer should be strong and earnest in proportion to the greatness of the need; and sin is the worst of leprosies, separating the soul not only from men but from God, curable by none but Christ — so the sinner, conscious of his defilement, should cry as earnestly as these lepers, "have mercy on us." The witnesses note the spirit of the prayer: humble (they stand afar off, daring not to approach), believing (they own Christ as Master able to help), and importunate (lifting up their voices). The expositors draw the lesson for every soul under the leprosy of sin: to come to Christ in like manner — earnestly, humbly, believing, pleading only mercy — for he alone, with his pitying heart and powerful hand, can cleanse the defiled. The verse thus models the cry of faith from the depth of need: the afflicted, united in misery, lifting one voice to the Master for the mercy that he alone can give.
14:
"And when he saw them, he said unto them, Go shew yourselves unto the priests. And it came to pass, that, as they went, they were cleansed." The witnesses note Christ's command and the faith it required. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the testing nature of the order: "he gave them an implied assurance that they would be healed... this required no small measure of faith, for he did not first heal them and then tell them to go; he told them to go without expressly assuring them that they would be healed, and without as yet any evidence to show to the priest." Cyril notes the command sent them to the priests because the law required those cleansed of leprosy to be examined and to offer sacrifice (Leviticus 14); and "in bidding them go to the priests He meant nothing more than that they were just about to be healed." Theophylact notes the cleansing came "as they went" — in the act of obedience.

The witnesses note the lepers were healed not before they obeyed, but in the very act of obeying — "as they went, they were cleansed."

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansing of the ten through obedient faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ's blessing is given in the path of obedience, and that faith acts on his word before it sees the evidence. The command "Go shew yourselves unto the priests," the witnesses note, was an act of grace and a test of faith at once. It was gracious, for to send them to the priests — whose office was to certify a leper clean (Leviticus 14) — was an implied promise that they would be cleansed; but it was a test, the expositors press, for Christ did not first heal them and then send them, but bade them go while still leprous, with no visible change to show the priest, requiring them to set out on the strength of his bare word. And the lepers believed: they went, and "as they went, they were cleansed" — the healing meeting them in the very act of obedience, not before. The witnesses draw the rich lesson: so the sinner, defiled with sin's leprosy, is bidden to come to Christ and obey his word, trusting his power to cleanse though as yet he sees no evidence; and the cleansing is given to such believing obedience. Barnes presses the parallel: the soul should put faith in Christ and follow his directions with full confidence that he can and will heal, and that in due time it shall have the fullest evidence of its peace with God. The expositors note the order: faith acts first, on the word; the blessing follows, in the path of obedience — for had the lepers stood still, demanding to see themselves healed before they would go, they would have remained lepers. The verse thus teaches that Christ's grace is laid hold of by an obedient faith that takes him at his word and acts upon it; and the ten, cleansed as they went, are the witnesses that those who obey the word of Christ in faith find his healing power meeting them on the way.
15:
"And one of them, when he saw that he was healed, turned back, and with a loud voice glorified God." The witnesses note the one leper's grateful return. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the order of his actions: "sensible of the power of God and grateful for his mercies, he returned to express his gratitude to God... Instead of obeying at once the letter of the command, he first expressed his thanks to God and to his Great Benefactor." Barnes notes this did not mean he disobeyed: "there is no evidence that he did not, after he had given thanks, go to the priest"; but his first impulse was to return and praise. He draws the lesson: "the first duty of sinners, after they have been forgiven and have the hope of eternal life, is to prostrate themselves at the feet of their Great Benefactor, and to consecrate themselves to his service."

The witnesses note the one, perceiving his cleansing, turns back glorifying God aloud — gratitude overflowing before he completes the journey to the priest.

So the witnesses make the verse the model of grateful response to Christ's mercy. The lesson the expositors draw is that the first movement of a heart truly sensible of grace is to return and give thanks. The one leper, the witnesses note, "when he saw that he was healed" — perceiving the cleansing in his own body — did not hurry on with the rest to claim his certificate, but "turned back, and with a loud voice glorified God." The expositors press the order of his feeling: gratitude came first; before the formal duty of showing himself to the priest, his heart turned to praise, and he came back to glorify God and (as the next verse shows) to fall at Christ's feet. Barnes is careful to note, the witnesses observe, that this was no neglect of Christ's command: doubtless he went to the priest afterward, as he must to be restored to society; but the impulse of his cleansed heart was first of all to thank his Healer. The expositors draw the searching lesson: so the sinner, cleansed from the leprosy of sin and given the hope of life, should make his first act the giving of thanks — prostrating himself before his Benefactor, glorifying God for the mercy received, and consecrating himself to his service, before going on to the duties of his new life. The witnesses note the loud voice: as the lepers had cried aloud for mercy, so now the one cries aloud in praise — the same earnestness that marked his prayer now marking his thanksgiving. The verse thus sets before us the grateful soul: perceiving the mercy, it turns back to its source, glorifies God openly, and seeks the feet of its Saviour — the response that the nine, hurrying on without a thought of thanks, so sadly lacked, and which Christ will single out for honour.
16:
"And fell down on his face at his feet, giving him thanks: and he was a Samaritan." The witnesses note the depth of the man's worship and the surprise of his nation. The Reformed Gill notes that, being cleansed, "he might draw nigh unto Jesus; and which he did, with the most profound respect... prostrated himself in this manner before him." Adam Clarke marks the irony of his being a Samaritan: "one who professed a very corrupt religion, and from whom much less was to be expected than from the other nine, who probably were Jews." Ellicott presses Luke's purpose: as in the parable of the Good Samaritan, "St. Luke's purpose falls in with the Catholicity of his Gospel, the breaking down of every middle wall of partition" — and notes the calamity of leprosy had for a time broken down the barrier between Jew and Samaritan.

The witnesses note the climax of the verse: the one who returned to worship at Christ's feet "was a Samaritan" — a despised foreigner, where the nine were of the covenant people.

So the witnesses make the verse the surprising revelation of the grateful one's nation. The lesson the expositors draw is the breadth of grace and the rebuke of covenant privilege without gratitude. The cleansed leper, the witnesses note, having returned glorifying God, now "fell down on his face at his feet, giving him thanks" — the posture of deepest worship and reverence, prostrate before his Healer, owning him as more than a man. And then comes the stroke that gives the story its point: "and he was a Samaritan." The expositors press the irony Clarke marks: the Samaritan held a corrupt and despised religion, was reckoned by the Jews little better than a heathen, and from him least of all might gratitude have been expected; yet he, and not the nine Jews, returned to give thanks. The witnesses observe Ellicott's note on Luke's purpose: the evangelist, here as in the parable of the Good Samaritan, delights to break down the wall between Jew and Gentile, showing grace reaching beyond Israel and faith found where it was least looked for. The expositors draw the searching lesson: covenant privilege is no substitute for a grateful heart; the nine, who had the law and the promises, went their way thankless, while the despised stranger alone returned to worship — a rebuke to all who presume on their religious standing yet render God no thanks. The witnesses note the worship itself: the Samaritan does not merely thank Christ as a benefactor, but falls on his face at his feet, an act of adoration that hints at a faith owning Christ as divine. The verse thus crowns the grateful one's return with the surprising disclosure of his nation, pressing the lesson that grace is wide and gratitude rare — and that a thankful Samaritan may put to shame nine privileged sons of the covenant.
17:
"And Jesus answering said, Were there not ten cleansed? but where are the nine?" The witnesses note Christ's searching question and its lament over ingratitude. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the reproof: "Jesus had commanded them to go to the priest, and they were probably literally obeying the commandment; but they were impatient to be healed and selfish in wishing it, and had no gratitude to God or their Benefactor." He notes Christ did not forbid their gratitude to him but "rather seems to reprove them for not" returning. Barnes presses the abiding application: "With what singular propriety may this question now be asked, Where are the nine? ... When men are restored from dangerous sickness, here and there one comes to give thanks to God; but where are the nine?"

The witnesses note the contrast Christ marks: all ten were cleansed, but only one returned to give thanks — "where are the nine?"

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's lament over human ingratitude. The lesson the expositors draw is the commonness of ingratitude even toward the greatest mercies, and Christ's grief over it. The question "where are the nine?" the witnesses note, is at once a reproof and a lament: all ten had received the same great mercy, all ten were cleansed of an incurable disease, yet nine went their way without a word of thanks, and only one returned. The expositors press the indictment: the nine were not ignorant of who had healed them, nor unable to return; they were, Barnes notes, impatient and selfish, eager for the cure but careless of the Healer, content to receive the benefit and forget the Benefactor. And Christ's question, the witnesses observe, shows that he marks and is grieved by such ingratitude: he does not let it pass unnoticed, but draws attention to it, that the one might be honoured and the nine rebuked. The expositors draw the searching application Barnes presses: this is a striking illustration of human nature, for ingratitude toward God is the common case — of the many who receive mercies, healing, deliverance, daily good, how few return to give thanks; the one grateful soul is the exception, the nine thankless the rule. The witnesses note the lesson for every heart: to receive God's mercies and render no thanks is to be of the nine, and to incur the Saviour's reproof; while to return, like the one, with praise and worship, is to please him and be honoured by him. The verse thus turns the miracle into a mirror, in which every reader may see the human tendency to forget the Giver in the gift, and hear Christ's grieved question, "where are the nine?" — a call to be of the grateful one, not of the thankless many.
18:
"There are not found that returned to give glory to God, save this stranger." The witnesses note the contrast between the thankful stranger and the thankless covenant people. Adam Clarke draws the lesson: "Often God receives more praise and affectionate obedience from those who had long lived without his knowledge and fear, than from those who were bred up among his people; the simple reason is, those who have much forgiven will love much" (Luke 7:47). Ellicott and Robertson note the word "stranger" (allogenēs, "a man of another race, an alien") was "probably a term of contempt in common use among the Jews," implying the Samaritan "was not recognised as being, in any sense, a child of Abraham."

The witnesses note the pointed word: only "this stranger," the alien, the despised foreigner, returned to give glory to God — the nine of Israel did not.

So the witnesses make the verse the rebuke of privileged ingratitude by the gratitude of the outcast. The lesson the expositors draw is that grace is often most owned by those who once stood farthest from it, and that covenant standing without thankfulness profits nothing. The witnesses note the sting in the word "stranger": Christ uses the very term of contempt by which the Jews despised the Samaritan — the alien, the man of another race, no child of Abraham in their reckoning — and lays it as a rebuke upon the nine; for this despised outsider alone "returned to give glory to God," while the privileged sons of the covenant went their way unthankful. The expositors press Clarke's observation: God often receives more love and praise from those who long lived in ignorance of him than from those bred among his people, for those who have much forgiven love much, while those who presume on long privilege grow cold and careless. The witnesses observe the recurring keynote of Luke's Gospel: grace reaching beyond Israel, faith and gratitude found among the despised, the last put before the first. The expositors draw the searching application: it is a fearful thing to have the form of religion, the heritage of the covenant, the outward privileges of God's people, and yet to render him no thanks — to be of the nine; while the stranger, who had least, by his gratitude is honoured above them all. The verse thus presses the lesson that what God prizes is not privilege but a thankful heart; that the despised outsider who glorifies God is dearer to him than the privileged who forget him; and that gratitude, not lineage or standing, marks the soul that truly knows the grace it has received.
19:
"And he said unto him, Arise, go thy way: thy faith hath made thee whole." The witnesses note the Samaritan's fuller blessing, beyond the cleansing he shared with the nine. The Reformed Gill notes the man "lay at his feet upon his face, adoring and praising him; nor did he attempt to rise till Jesus bid him." The witnesses observe that all ten were "cleansed" of their leprosy, but to this one alone Christ says, "thy faith hath made thee whole" — a word, the expositors note, suggesting a deeper healing than the body's: the salvation of the soul, granted to the faith that returned to worship. The grateful Samaritan, who came back believing and adoring, receives not only the cleansing of his flesh but the assurance of being "made whole" — saved.

The witnesses note the distinction: the nine were cleansed; the one is "made whole" — his faith bringing him a blessing beyond his body's cure.

So the witnesses make the verse the crowning of the grateful Samaritan with a salvation the nine did not receive. The lesson the expositors draw is that gratitude and faith carry the soul beyond the outward mercy to the deeper blessing of salvation. All ten lepers, the witnesses note, received the same bodily cleansing — the gift was common to the grateful and the thankless alike, for God's providential mercies fall on the unthankful too. But to this one, the expositors press, Christ adds a word he gives to none of the nine: "thy faith hath made thee whole" — and the phrase, the same Christ used to the saved woman (Luke 7:50) and others, points beyond the body to the soul; the Samaritan, who returned in faith and fell worshipping at Christ's feet, is "made whole" in the fuller sense, his faith bringing him not only a healed body but a saved soul. The witnesses observe the lesson: the nine had their leprosy cleansed, but went away with no more; the one, by his faith and gratitude, received the greater gift, the salvation that faith lays hold of. The expositors draw the searching application: many receive God's outward mercies — health, deliverance, daily good — without faith or thanks, and have nothing beyond the temporal benefit; but the soul that, like the Samaritan, returns to Christ in believing gratitude, finds in him not the lesser gift only but the greater, the wholeness of salvation. Gill's tender note, the witnesses observe, marks the man's posture: he lay worshipping at Christ's feet, rising only at his word — the very picture of faith's humble adoration, which Christ honours with the word of salvation. The verse thus closes the account of the ten lepers by distinguishing the grateful believer from the thankless many: cleansed bodies the nine received and forgot; but the one, who returned in faith, was "made whole" — sent on his way saved, the lasting blessing answering his faith and his thanks.
20:
"And when he was demanded of the Pharisees, when the kingdom of God should come, he answered them and said, The kingdom of God cometh not with observation." The witnesses note the Pharisees' question and Christ's answer. Cyril hears mockery in the question: "as though they said tauntingly, Before the kingdom of God come, which you speak of, the death of the cross will be your lot"; yet Christ, "when reviled reviles not again," answers patiently that "the kingdom comes not with observation" — that is, "seek not to know the time when the kingdom of heaven shall be at hand, for that time can be observed neither by men nor angels, not as the time of the Incarnation, proclaimed by the Prophets and the Angels." Cyril adds the Pharisees may have asked "because they thought that on our Lord's coming into Jerusalem, the kingdom of God would be immediately manifested."

The witnesses note Christ's correction of the carnal expectation: the kingdom does not come "with observation" — with outward show, visible pomp, or signs to be watched for.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's correction of false notions of the kingdom. The lesson the expositors draw is that the kingdom of God is not an outward, observable, pompous thing, as the Jews imagined. The Pharisees, the witnesses note, asked "when the kingdom of God should come" — expecting, in their carnal hope, a visible earthly kingdom that would be set up with display and signs, perhaps the moment the Messiah entered Jerusalem; and their question may have carried a taunt, doubting that one bound for the cross could establish any kingdom. Christ answers, the expositors press, by overturning their whole expectation: "the kingdom of God cometh not with observation" — it does not arrive with outward pomp and ceremony, with marching armies or visible glory that men may watch for and point to; its coming is not to be calculated by signs in the sky or events to be observed. Cyril's note, the witnesses observe, draws the contrast with the Incarnation: that first coming was heralded by prophets and angels, marked by signs men could observe; but the kingdom Christ speaks of comes otherwise — spiritually, inwardly, without the external spectacle the Pharisees craved. The expositors draw the lesson against all carnal and date-setting expectations of the kingdom: it is not a matter of outward observation, of watching for visible signs or fixing times, but a spiritual reality that establishes itself quietly in the hearts and among the people of God. The verse thus opens the discourse on the kingdom and the coming of the Son of man by correcting the error at its root — that the kingdom of God is an observable, outward thing — and prepares for the deeper truth that the next verse declares: that the kingdom is within, or among, them, already present in their midst in the person and work of Christ.
21:
"Neither shall they say, Lo here! or, lo there! for, behold, the kingdom of God is within you." The witnesses note the two readings of "within you." Albert Barnes lays out both: "this is capable of two interpretations. 1st. The reign of God is in the heart — it does not come with pomp and splendour... but reigns in the heart" by its renewing power; "2nd," the phrase may mean "among you" or "in the midst of you" — the kingdom, in the person of the Messiah and his work, was already present among them. Barnes also notes the rebuke of the false Christs who said "lo here, lo there," pointing men to this place or that for signs (cf. Acts 5:36-37).

The witnesses note Christ's denial that the kingdom may be located by pointing — "lo here, lo there" — and his declaration that it is already "within" or "among" them.

So the witnesses make the verse the great correction of where the kingdom is to be found. The lesson the expositors draw is that the kingdom of God is not an outward, locatable thing to be pointed to, but a present reality — whether read as inward in the heart or present in their midst. The witnesses note Christ's first denial: men shall not say "Lo here! or, lo there!" — the kingdom is not to be located in this place or that, as though one could point to its arrival; this rebukes both the carnal expectation of a visible kingdom and the false Christs who would lead men to seek it in deserts and mountains. Then comes the positive word, "the kingdom of God is within you" — and the expositors weigh its two senses. The first, the witnesses note, takes "within you" of the heart: the kingdom is a spiritual reign, set up not with outward show but in the inward man, ruling the heart by grace, peace, and righteousness; it comes silently, transforming souls, not striking the senses. The second takes the phrase as "among you" or "in the midst of you": the kingdom was already present in the person of Christ the King and his work among them, so that they need not look for it elsewhere — it stood before them, though they perceived it not. The expositors note that both readings press the same truth against the Pharisees' error: the kingdom is not a future, outward, observable thing to be pointed to, but a present spiritual reality, already begun in Christ and in the hearts of his people. The witnesses observe the irony in the second reading: the Pharisees asked when the kingdom would come, while the King himself stood in their midst — the kingdom was "among them," and they knew it not. The verse thus completes Christ's correction of the carnal hope: the kingdom of God is not to be located by pointing, for it is within and among them — a spiritual reign already present in the King and his grace, which men must perceive not with the outward eye but with the heart.
22:
"And he said unto the disciples, The days will come, when ye shall desire to see one of the days of the Son of man, and ye shall not see it." The witnesses note the turn to the disciples and the coming days of trial. Cyril reads it of the persecution ahead: Christ "would fain prepare His disciples for suffering... He therefore foretells that before His coming from heaven at the end of the world, persecution will break out upon them; so terrible will be the persecution that they would desire to see one of His days, that is, of that time when they yet walked with Christ." Theophylact agrees: "their life was then without trouble, for Christ took care of them; but the time was coming when Christ should be taken away, and they should be exposed to perils, brought before kings and princes, and then they should long for the first time and its tranquillity."

The witnesses note the prophecy of coming distress: the disciples, soon to lose Christ's visible presence and face persecution, would long for the peaceful days they had known with him, and not see them again.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's forewarning of the disciples' coming tribulation. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the peaceful days of Christ's presence and the trials that would follow his departure. Christ, the witnesses note, turns from the Pharisees to his disciples with a sobering word: "the days will come, when ye shall desire to see one of the days of the Son of man, and ye shall not see it." The fathers read this, the expositors press, of the time after Christ's ascension, when the disciples, deprived of his visible care and exposed to fierce persecution — brought before kings and councils, hunted and afflicted — would look back with longing on the tranquil days when they walked with him, and wish for even one such day again, but find it gone. Theophylact's observation, the witnesses observe, marks the change: while Christ was with them, their life was sheltered, for he took care of and protected them; but with him taken away, they would face perils alone (humanly speaking), and in the midst of their sufferings would yearn for the lost peace of his presence. The expositors note the tenderness and the realism of the warning: Christ does not hide from his disciples the hardship to come, but forewarns them, that they may be prepared and not overwhelmed; yet he also implicitly comforts them, for the very longing for "the days of the Son of man" looks forward to his coming again. The verse thus prepares the disciples for the loss of Christ's visible presence and the tribulation that would follow — teaching them to value the days of grace while they have them, and steeling them for the trials in which they would long, in vain, for the peaceful fellowship they had once enjoyed with their Lord.
23:
"And they shall say to you, See here; or, see there: go not after them, nor follow them." The witnesses note the warning against false Christs and deceivers. The Reformed Gill explains the cry "see here, or see there": it shall be said by one or another that Christ is in this place or that, "and he will quickly appear, and deliver the people of the Jews out of all their distresses by the Romans" — the claims of false messiahs in the troubled times before Jerusalem's fall. Barnes notes that "many false Christs, according to Josephus, appeared about that time, attempting to lead away the people" (cf. Matthew 24:23-26). Ellicott observes the near-identity with Matthew 24:23, the same teaching reproduced in a different setting.

The witnesses note Christ's flat command: when men point to this place or that, claiming the Messiah is there, the disciples are "not to go after them, nor follow them."

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's warning against the deceivers who would arise. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disciples, in the days of distress, must not be led astray by those who localize and announce false comings of the Christ. In the troubled times before Jerusalem's destruction, the witnesses note, and before the true coming of the Son of man, many would arise crying "see here; or, see there" — pointing to this leader or that place, claiming the Messiah had appeared to deliver the nation; and Josephus, the expositors observe (cited by Barnes), records that many such false Christs did arise, leading multitudes after them to their ruin. Against these Christ gives a plain command: "go not after them, nor follow them" — the disciples are not to be deceived by the localized claims, the pointing to here and there, the promises of a hidden Messiah to be sought in deserts or secret places. The expositors press the connection to the foregoing: since the kingdom comes not with observation, and the true coming of the Son of man will be (as the next verse declares) sudden and universally visible as lightning, any claim that points to a particular spot — "see here, see there" — is by that very mark false; the real coming will need no such announcement, for none could miss it. The witnesses draw the abiding lesson: God's people, in every age of expectation and distress, are to beware of those who would fix the place or the secret hiding of the Christ, and not run after such claims; for the true coming will be unmistakable and universal, not a thing whispered of one corner. The verse thus arms the disciples against deception in the days of longing and trial: when impostors point here and there, they are to stand fast and follow none, knowing that the Son of man, when he comes, will come as the lightning that fills the whole sky.
24:
"For as the lightning, that lighteneth out of the one part under heaven, shineth unto the other part under heaven; so shall also the Son of man be in his day." The witnesses note the suddenness and universal visibility of the true coming. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the point: "the second coming will be sudden and universally visible. There are still some poor souls who are waiting" for a secret coming, but the lightning that flashes across the whole sky leaves no room for "see here, see there." Ellicott notes Luke's vivid form — "the two parts under heaven" for Matthew's "east" and "west," and "lighteneth" and "shineth" more pictorial than Matthew's words — and that "the day of the Son of man" here replaces Matthew's "coming" (parousia).

The witnesses note the figure of the lightning: flashing from one side of heaven to the other, sudden, brilliant, seen by all at once — so shall the Son of man be in his day.

So the witnesses make the verse the great answer to the deceivers' "see here, see there." The lesson the expositors draw is that the coming of the Son of man will be sudden, universal, and unmistakable, like the lightning. The witnesses note the aptness of the figure: lightning needs no announcement, no pointing to a place; it flashes across the whole expanse of heaven, from one part to the other, seen at once by all — sudden, brilliant, undeniable. So, Christ declares, "shall also the Son of man be in his day": his coming will not be a hidden thing whispered of one corner, to be sought "here" or "there," but a manifestation as sudden and as universally visible as the lightning, which none can miss and none can localize. The expositors press the force against the false Christs: the very secrecy and localization of their claims — "see here, see there" — prove them false, for the true coming will be open to all the world at once. Robertson's observation, the witnesses note, applies the lesson still: those who look for a secret, partial coming of Christ mistake his word, for the lightning leaves no room for such expectation. The expositors observe the double reference many find in the whole discourse: the figure suits both the coming of the Son of man in judgment on Jerusalem (sudden, sweeping, undeniable) and his final coming at the end of the world (visible to all, the great and universal manifestation). The witnesses note the comfort and the warning together: the suddenness means the disciples must be ever ready, not deceived by impostors nor lulled by delay; and the universality means the coming will vindicate Christ before all the world. The verse thus sets the true coming of the Son of man over against every false and localized claim: sudden as the lightning, visible from one part of heaven to the other — a coming that needs no herald and admits no doubt, before which the deceivers' "here" and "there" are exposed as lies.
25:
"But first must he suffer many things, and be rejected of this generation." The witnesses note the prediction of the cross set within the discourse of the coming. The grammatical-historical Robertson states it plainly: "the second coming will be only after the Cross." Ellicott marks the significance of the interposition: "the interposition of this prophecy of the Passion in a discourse which bears primarily on the Second Advent is an individualising feature of this record of St. Luke's" (cf. Matthew 16:21; 17:22). Clarke connects it to the judgment: "as the cup of the iniquity of this people shall not be full till they have finally rejected and crucified the Lord of life, so this desolation cannot take place till after my death."

The witnesses note the order Christ sets: before the glory of his coming, "first must he suffer" — the cross precedes the crown, the rejection precedes the revelation.

So the witnesses make the verse the necessary cross before the coming glory. The lesson the expositors draw is that the suffering of Christ must precede his glorious coming, and that the cross is the appointed way to the crown. The witnesses note the abrupt but deliberate interposition: in the midst of speaking of the day of the Son of man, sudden and glorious as lightning, Christ adds, "but first must he suffer many things, and be rejected of this generation." The expositors press the order this establishes: before the glory, the cross; before the coming in power, the rejection and death; the Son of man who will one day flash across the heavens must "first" be despised, rejected, and crucified by the very generation among whom he stood. Robertson's terse summary, the witnesses observe, captures it: the second coming comes only after the cross. Ellicott's note marks this as a feature peculiar to Luke, the evangelist weaving the prophecy of the Passion into the discourse on the Advent — a reminder that the path to glory runs through suffering. Clarke's observation, the witnesses note, links the cross to the judgment that the discourse foretells: the desolation of Jerusalem could not come until the nation had filled the cup of its iniquity by rejecting and crucifying its Lord; the suffering of Christ both precedes his coming in glory and seals the doom of those who reject him. The expositors draw the lesson: the disciples, longing for the day of the Son of man, must not forget that it lies beyond the cross — that their Master must suffer and be rejected before he is revealed; and so must they themselves take up the cross before the crown. The verse thus grounds the coming glory in the prior suffering: the Son of man, sudden and glorious in his day, must first be rejected and slain — the cross the appointed road to the coming, for the King and for his people alike.
26:
"And as it was in the days of Noe, so shall it be also in the days of the Son of man." The witnesses note the comparison with Noah's day and its lesson of careless security. Bede sets it: "the coming of our Lord, which He had compared to lightning, He now likens to the days of Noah and Lot, when a sudden destruction came upon mankind." Chrysostom traces the cause of the antediluvians' ruin: "for refusing to believe the words of warning they were suddenly visited with punishment; but their unbelief proceeded from self-indulgence and softness of mind, for such as a man's wishes are, will also be his expectations." Ambrose guards the lesson: "let it not be supposed that marriages, or meat and drink, are condemned... but moderation is to be sought in all things; for whatsoever is more than this is of evil."

The witnesses note the parallel: as the men of Noah's day lived in careless ease, heedless of warning, until the flood swept them away, so shall it be at the coming of the Son of man.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning from the days of Noah. The lesson the expositors draw is that the coming of the Son of man will find the world in careless security, as the flood found the antediluvians. The witnesses note the point of the comparison, which the following verses unfold: the men of Noah's day were not destroyed for great and unusual crimes only, but were absorbed in the ordinary business and pleasures of life — eating, drinking, marrying — heedless of the warning Noah preached, until the flood came suddenly and swept them all away. So, Christ declares, shall it be at his coming: the world will be sunk in careless ease, occupied with its affairs and pleasures, unbelieving and unprepared, when the day breaks upon it. Chrysostom's insight, the expositors press, lays bare the root of their ruin: their unbelief sprang from self-indulgence and softness — for a man's desires shape his expectations, and those who love their ease will not believe in coming judgment, but put the evil day far off. Ambrose's caution, the witnesses observe, guards the lesson from excess: it is not eating, drinking, or marriage that is condemned — these are lawful and necessary — but the immoderate, godless absorption in them that crowds out all thought of God and judgment; the sin was not living, but living as if there were no God and no reckoning. The expositors note the double reference the discourse carries: the warning suits both the sudden judgment on Jerusalem, which fell on a careless and unbelieving nation, and the final coming of the Son of man, which will find the world likewise absorbed and unprepared. The verse thus presses upon every age the lesson of Noah's day: that the coming will be sudden, and the world careless; and that the disciple must not be lulled by the ordinary round of life into forgetting the day that comes, but watch and be ready, lest the flood of judgment find him, like the antediluvians, heedless and unprepared.
27:
"They did eat, they drank, they married wives, they were given in marriage, until the day that Noe entered into the ark, and the flood came, and destroyed them all." The witnesses note the carelessness of the antediluvians and the suddenness of their ruin. The Reformed Gill clarifies the sin: not eating and drinking "in a common way, with moderation... which is not blameworthy," but living "in an extravagant and luxurious manner, indulging their sensual appetites, and putting away the evil day far from them, that Noah told them of." Clarke presses the heedlessness: "they spent their whole lives in reference to this world, and made no sort of provision for their immortal souls; so it was when the Romans came to destroy Judea — universal carelessness, and no one regarded the warnings." Ellicott notes the continuous tense — "they were eating and drinking" — implying habitual absorption, and that the Greek word for "flood" is always used of Noah's deluge.

The witnesses note the contrast: the careless round of life went on "until the day" Noah entered the ark — and then, suddenly, the flood came and destroyed them all.

So the witnesses make the verse the picture of careless absorption overtaken by sudden destruction. The lesson the expositors draw is that worldly security, heedless of warning, ends in sudden and total ruin. The antediluvians, the witnesses note, "did eat, they drank, they married" — went on with the ordinary, lawful business of life, but (as Gill marks) in a spirit of luxurious self-indulgence, sunk in their appetites, and above all putting far from them the evil day of which Noah, the preacher of righteousness, had warned them. Their sin, the expositors press, was not that they ate or married, but that they did so in godless carelessness, living wholly for this world, making no provision for their souls, and ignoring the warning of coming judgment. And the ruin, the witnesses observe, was sudden and complete: the careless round continued "until the day that Noe entered into the ark" — and on that very day "the flood came, and destroyed them all"; the security that had mocked the warning was swept away in a moment, none escaping. Clarke's parallel, the expositors note, applies it to Jerusalem: the same universal carelessness reigned when the Romans came, the nation heedless of Christ's warnings, until destruction fell. The witnesses draw the abiding lesson: the danger is not in the common affairs of life, but in being so absorbed in them as to forget God and the coming reckoning; the antediluvians perished not for unusual wickedness but for unprepared worldliness, overtaken in their security. The verse thus warns every generation, and points to the final coming: as the flood found the world careless and destroyed it suddenly, so the day of the Son of man will break upon a heedless world; and the disciple must not be of those who eat and drink and forget the day, but must watch, lest the sudden judgment find him absorbed in the world and unprepared for God.
28:
"Likewise also as it was in the days of Lot; they did eat, they drank, they bought, they sold, they planted, they builded." The witnesses note the second example, from Sodom, and its added activities. The Reformed Gill sets the time: "when Lot lived in Sodom, before and at the time of the destruction of that city." Barnes draws out the heedlessness: "they were busy in the affairs of this life, as if nothing were about to happen"; and he notes the lesson of Sodom's overthrow, that "God will punish the guilty" (Genesis 19; Jude 7). The witnesses note the parallel to Noah's day, with new activities named — buying, selling, planting, building — the settled pursuits of a society that thought itself secure.

The witnesses note the same careless absorption in Sodom as in Noah's day, now extended to commerce and building — the marks of a people planning for a long future, heedless of judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the second warning, from the days of Lot. The lesson the expositors draw is that the same careless, worldly security that doomed the antediluvians reigned in Sodom, and will mark the coming of the Son of man. The witnesses note that Christ adds a second example to reinforce the first: as in Noah's day, so "in the days of Lot," the people were absorbed in the ordinary business of life — and here the list is fuller, adding to eating and drinking the activities of "buying, selling, planting, building." The expositors press the significance of these added pursuits: buying and selling are the works of commerce, planting and building the works of men who look for a long and settled future; the Sodomites were not merely indulging appetite but laying plans, investing, constructing — busy in the affairs of this life "as if nothing were about to happen," utterly heedless of the doom about to fall. Their sin, the witnesses observe (as with Noah's generation), was not in the lawful activities themselves, but in the godless security that pursued them with no thought of judgment — and, in Sodom's case, joined to the gross wickedness for which the city was infamous. The expositors note the force of the example: Sodom's overthrow stands in Scripture as a perpetual proof that God will surely punish the guilty (Jude 7), and a warning to all who live as if there were no reckoning. The witnesses draw the lesson, pressed by the whole passage: the coming of the Son of man will find men so occupied — eating, trading, building for a future they presume upon — careless of the day that comes. The verse thus doubles the warning of Noah's day with the example of Lot: the same heedless absorption in the world, the same planning for a long earthly future, the same security that mocks the warning — all overtaken, as the next verse shows, by sudden destruction; that the disciple may learn to sit loose to the world and watch for his Lord.
29:
"But the same day that Lot went out of Sodom it rained fire and brimstone from heaven, and destroyed them all." The witnesses note the suddenness of Sodom's destruction and the rescue of the righteous. The Reformed Gill notes Lot was "plucked and brought from thence by the angels early in the morning... and a fine morning it was," before the fire fell. Adam Clarke presses the scriptural exactness: "instead of it rained, Genesis 19:24 justifies the insertion of the pronoun he... for it is there said that Jehovah rained fire and brimstone from Jehovah out of heaven." Ellicott notes the combination "fire and brimstone" comes from the Septuagint of Genesis 19:24 and recurs in Revelation (14:10; 20:10; 21:8).

The witnesses note the timing: "the same day" Lot escaped, the fire fell — the righteous rescued, and then, at once, the destruction of all the rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the sudden destruction of Sodom and the deliverance of Lot. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold: the suddenness and totality of judgment on the careless wicked, and the certain rescue of the righteous before it falls. The witnesses note the precise timing Christ marks: "the same day that Lot went out of Sodom it rained fire and brimstone" — the very day, indeed (as the next verses imply) the very hour, of Lot's escape saw the fire fall; the careless security of the city, busy with buying and building, was overtaken the instant the righteous man was safe. The expositors press the suddenness: as with the flood, so with Sodom, the blow fell without further delay once the righteous were withdrawn — "destroyed them all," none of the heedless escaping. Clarke's note on the text, the witnesses observe, draws out a striking detail: it was "Jehovah" who rained fire "from Jehovah out of heaven" (Genesis 19:24) — a phrase the fathers and many since have read as a hint of the plurality in the Godhead, the LORD on earth and the LORD in heaven.
