But the lesson Christ presses, the expositors note, is the pattern of judgment and deliverance: the wicked, careless to the last, are suddenly and wholly destroyed, while the righteous are first removed to safety — Lot plucked out by the angels before a single spark fell. The witnesses observe the comfort hidden in the warning: God does not destroy the righteous with the wicked, but delivers them first, as he delivered Noah in the ark and Lot from Sodom; so the disciple, however the judgment falls on a careless world, is safe in God's keeping. The verse thus completes the example of Lot with the same double truth as Noah's: sudden, total ruin for the heedless wicked, and sure deliverance for the righteous — a pattern that the coming of the Son of man, in judgment on Jerusalem and at the last day, will repeat.
30:
"Even thus shall it be in the day when the Son of man is revealed." The witnesses note the application of both examples and the double reference of the day. The Reformed Gill draws the parallel and applies it to both judgments: "as in the days of Noah and Lot men lived in great carnality and security, thoughtless and fearless of danger, so were the Jews before the destruction of their city and temple... and such will be the times of indolence and supineness before the coming of the day of the Lord to judgment." He notes that, as Noah and Lot were saved, so "when the Christians removed from Jerusalem and went to Pella" they were preserved — the righteous again delivered before the blow.

The witnesses note Christ draws the application: "even thus" — as in the days of Noah and Lot — "shall it be in the day when the Son of man is revealed."

So the witnesses make the verse the application of the two examples to the coming of the Son of man. The lesson the expositors draw is that the day of Christ's revelation will repeat the pattern of Noah's and Lot's days: a careless world overtaken by sudden judgment, with the righteous delivered. The witnesses note the force of "even thus": Christ gathers up both examples — the flood and the fire — and applies them directly to "the day when the Son of man is revealed." The expositors press the double reference that Gill and many find in the whole discourse: the warning suits, first, the coming of the Son of man in judgment on Jerusalem (AD 70), which fell on a careless, secure, unbelieving nation, busy to the last with its affairs, while the Christians, warned beforehand, withdrew to Pella and were saved; and, second, the final coming of Christ in judgment at the end of the world, which will likewise find mankind absorbed in worldly security, and will deliver his people and destroy the heedless. The witnesses observe that both fulfillments share the pattern the examples teach: careless absorption, sudden destruction, the rescue of the righteous. The expositors draw the lesson: the disciple is not to be lulled, as Jerusalem was, into thinking the day far off, but to watch and be ready; for the revelation of the Son of man, whether in the judgment of a city or the judgment of the world, will break suddenly upon a heedless people, and only those who are God's, withdrawn from the world's security and prepared, will be delivered. The "revealed," the witnesses note, marks the day as the unveiling of the Son of man, now hidden, then made manifest in power — answering the lightning of verse 24, sudden and unmistakable. The verse thus drives home the application of Noah and Lot: as their days, so the day of the Son of man — careless world, sudden judgment, delivered righteous — a warning to watch and a promise of deliverance, fulfilled in the fall of Jerusalem and to be fulfilled fully at the last.
31:
"In that day, he which shall be upon the housetop, and his stuff in the house, let him not come down to take it away: and he that is in the field, let him likewise not return back." The witnesses note the command of urgent flight and its spiritual reading. Ambrose reads the housetop spiritually: "he is on the housetop who, departing from carnal things, rises to the summit of the highest virtues; let him not fall back to the groveling business of this world." Augustine develops it: "he is on the housetop who, departing from carnal things, breathes the free air of a spiritual life; but the vessels in the house are the carnal senses" — let the spiritual man not descend again to take pleasure in carnal life; and "he that is in the field" — labouring in the Church, as Paul planting and Apollos watering — "let him not look back upon the worldly prospects which he has renounced."

The witnesses note the literal urgency: when the day comes, flight must be so swift that none may turn back even for his goods.

So the witnesses make the verse the command of immediate, unencumbered flight. The lesson the expositors draw is the urgency of escape in the day of judgment, and, spiritually, the renouncing of the world for the soul's safety. The literal sense, the witnesses note, fitted the flight from Jerusalem: when the Roman armies came, the believer on the housetop must not come down to gather his goods, nor he in the field return home for his possessions, but flee at once, for delay would be death — and so the Christians, heeding the warning, escaped while the city fell. The expositors press the principle: in the day of judgment, the soul's safety must override every earthly concern; he who pauses to save his property may lose his life. But the fathers, the witnesses observe, draw a deeper, spiritual lesson. Ambrose and Augustine read the housetop as the height of spiritual life, attained by departing from carnal things; and they warn the one who has risen there not to "come down" — not to descend again into the worldly business and fleshly pleasures he has renounced, lest in the day of trial he lose his soul. The "stuff in the house" and the "vessels" they read as the carnal senses and worldly goods, which the spiritual man must not return to take up. And "he that is in the field" — the labourer in God's work — must not "look back" to the worldly prospects he forsook, lest, like Lot's wife, he perish. The expositors note the converging lesson, literal and spiritual: whether fleeing Jerusalem or fleeing the world, the soul must press forward without turning back, holding the things of earth loosely, ready to leave all for safety. The verse thus commands an undivided, forward-pressing flight from the coming judgment — the body from the doomed city, and the soul from the world it has renounced — preparing for the sharp warning of the next verse, "Remember Lot's wife."
32:
"Remember Lot's wife." The witnesses note this brief, weighty warning. The Reformed Gill recounts the traditions and the fact: she "looked back to see what would be the end of her father's house," or because "her heart yearned after her daughters... upon which she became a pillar of salt" (Genesis 19:26). Barnes draws the lesson: "she looked back — she delayed — perhaps she desired to take something with her, and God made her a monument of his displeasure; Jesus directs us to remember her fate, and to learn that they who would be saved must wholly forsake all sin, and press onward without looking back." The witnesses note the pointed brevity of the command — a single, arresting sentence set in the midst of the discourse.

The witnesses note the warning is by example: "Remember Lot's wife" — recall what befell her who, fleeing Sodom, looked back, and learn from her doom.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn warning against looking back. The lesson the expositors draw is that the soul fleeing judgment must not look back to the world it leaves, on pain of sharing the doom it flees. The command, the witnesses note, is striking in its brevity — three words that arrest the hearer and drive home, by a single example, the lesson of the foregoing verse: do not turn back. Lot's wife, the expositors press, had been brought out of Sodom by the angels, set on the path of escape, almost safe; yet she "looked back" — whether from curiosity to see the city's fate, or from a heart that yearned after what she left, or from a lingering love of Sodom and reluctance to leave it wholly — and in that backward look she perished, turned to a pillar of salt, a perpetual monument of God's displeasure against those who, fleeing, cling to the doomed world. The witnesses observe Barnes's drawing of the lesson: she looked back, she delayed, perhaps she longed to save some possession — and so she was lost, almost out of danger yet destroyed; teaching that those who would be saved must forsake all sin wholly and press on without a backward glance. The expositors note the searching application: it is not enough to begin the flight from sin and the world; one must not look back, not linger, not let the heart remain where the feet have left. The half-hearted, who flee the judgment yet love the world they flee, who put their hand to the plough and look back (Luke 9:62), are in danger of Lot's wife's doom. The witnesses press the weight Christ lays on so brief a word: a single backward look, a divided heart, undid one who was nearly saved. The verse thus stands as a perpetual warning to every soul fleeing the wrath to come: remember her who looked back and perished, and forsake the world wholly, pressing forward without turning, lest the love of what is left behind become, as it did for her, the cause of ruin.
33:
"Whosoever shall seek to save his life shall lose it; and whosoever shall lose his life shall preserve it." The witnesses note the paradox of saving and losing life. The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws out the precise words: the better MSS. give for "save" a word rendered elsewhere "purchase" (Acts 20:28), suggesting "the man must purchase his lower life at the price of the higher, and he will be a loser by the bargain"; and for "preserve," a word better rendered "shall give life to it" (cf. 1 Timothy 6:13), "the man who is content to risk his natural life shall gain a life of a higher kind." Gill connects it to the flight: he who shall "seek to save his life by fleeing to some strong hold, or continuing in Jerusalem," shall lose it.

The witnesses note the paradox set in the context of flight and judgment: the self-preserving lose, the self-surrendering gain.

So the witnesses make the verse the great paradox of life saved and lost, applied to the day of judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that clinging to the present life for its own sake forfeits the higher life, while surrendering it for Christ secures it. The saying, the witnesses note, is one Christ uttered more than once, here set in the context of flight from judgment: in that day, "whosoever shall seek to save his life shall lose it" — he who, when the call to flee comes, clings to his earthly life and goods, lingering like Lot's wife to save what he has, or trusting in some refuge of his own, shall lose the very life he sought to keep; while "whosoever shall lose his life," who lets go his hold on earthly safety, forsaking all at Christ's word, "shall preserve it" — shall gain the higher and lasting life. The expositors press the deeper sense Ellicott draws from the words: the self-preserver tries to "purchase" his lower, natural life, but pays for it with the higher, eternal life, and is a loser by the bargain; the self-surrenderer, content to risk the natural life, is "given life" of a higher kind. The witnesses observe the connection to "Remember Lot's wife": she sought to save her life, or her hold on Sodom, and lost it; the lesson is the same — to grasp at the present life and the world is to forfeit the true life, while to let them go for Christ is to find it. The expositors draw the abiding application: in every age, those who make self-preservation and the love of this life their rule will lose all that matters, while those who, for Christ's sake, sit loose to life and the world, ready to surrender even life itself, will gain the life that endures. The verse thus crowns the warning of flight with its underlying principle: life is not saved by clinging but by surrendering — the paradox that he who would keep his life must lose it, and he who loses it for Christ shall keep it unto life eternal.
34:
"I tell you, in that night there shall be two men in one bed; the one shall be taken, and the other shall be left." The witnesses note the discriminating judgment and the meaning of "night." Ambrose reads the "night": "Antichrist is the hour of darkness... but Christ as lightning shines brightly, that we may see in that night the glory of the resurrection." Theophylact takes it of the suddenness — "Christ's coming will be in the night" — and presses that the example shows "not all the rich perish, nor all the poor are saved." Cyril reads "the two men in one bed" of "the rich who repose themselves in worldly pleasures, for a bed is a sign of rest; but not all who abound in riches are wicked — if one is good and elect in the faith, he will be taken, but another who is not so will be left."

The witnesses note the searching discrimination: two together, in the closest circumstances, yet at the coming one is taken and the other left.

So the witnesses make the verse the picture of the discriminating judgment at the coming of the Son of man. The lesson the expositors draw is that the day will divide those outwardly alike, separating the elect from the rest by an inward difference of faith, not outward condition. The figure, the witnesses note, is of two men "in one bed" — together, alike in circumstance, sharing the same rest — yet at the coming "the one shall be taken, and the other shall be left." The expositors press the point Theophylact and Cyril draw: the division does not run along the lines men reckon — rich and poor, high and low — for here are two in the same bed, the same outward lot, yet divided by judgment; not all the rich perish nor all the poor are saved, but the discrimination is according to faith and election. Cyril's reading, the witnesses observe, takes the bed as the symbol of worldly ease and repose, and notes that of two who share it, one may be "good and elect in the faith" and taken, the other not, and left — so that even among those at rest in the world, the judgment separates the believing from the unbelieving. The "night," the witnesses note, the fathers read both of the suddenness of the coming (which breaks upon the world as in the dark) and, spiritually (Ambrose), of the dark hour of trial and Antichrist, through which Christ's coming shines like lightning. The expositors weigh the meaning of "taken" and "left": most read "taken" as taken to safety and salvation (as Noah and Lot were taken out), "left" as left to destruction — though the figure's force lies less in the direction than in the separation. The witnesses draw the searching lesson: outward nearness, shared circumstance, even shared rest, will not save; in that day, two side by side will be divided, the one received and the other rejected, according to the hidden reality of faith. The verse thus warns that the coming will be a great sundering, dividing those most closely joined in this life, and pressing every soul to be of those taken, not left — ready, believing, and saved when the night of judgment falls.
35:
"Two women shall be grinding together; the one shall be taken, and the other left." The witnesses note the second example of the discriminating judgment. The grammatical-historical Robertson and Gill explain the scene: "two women grinding together" at one hand-mill, the grinding "the business of women," and "together" meaning in the same place, close beside each other (cf. Acts 2:1). Cyril of Jerusalem draws a pastoral comfort, anticipating the lowly hearer's fear: "I am but a woman, and I shall be taken at the mill: shall we then be despised? Be of good courage, O man; the Judge is no respecter of persons; He honours not the learned before the simple, nor the rich before the needy."

The witnesses note the second pair, women at the mill, again divided — one taken, one left — though engaged in the same humble labour.

So the witnesses make the verse the second instance of the great sundering. The lesson the expositors draw is that the discriminating judgment reaches every station, dividing even those joined in the same humble work. Christ, the witnesses note, adds a second example to the first: as two men in one bed, so "two women grinding together" — close beside each other at the same mill, sharing the same lowly task — yet "the one shall be taken, and the other left." The expositors press the same lesson as before: outward nearness and shared labour do not determine the judgment; two at the same mill, alike in occupation and circumstance, are divided by the coming, according to the inward difference of faith. The example, the witnesses observe, is drawn from the humblest of daily work — the women grinding grain at the hand-mill — to show that the judgment reaches every rank and occupation, the lowly as well as the great. Cyril of Jerusalem's pastoral application, the expositors note, is tender: the poor woman at the mill need not fear that her low station will cause her to be overlooked or despised; the Judge is no respecter of persons, honouring not the learned before the simple nor the rich before the needy, but judging by the heart and the faith. The witnesses draw the comfort and the warning together: the comfort, that the lowliest believer, found faithful at her humble task, will be taken and saved, her station no barrier; the warning, that mere occupation and nearness to others save no one — of two at the same mill, one is taken and one left. The expositors note how the repeated figure drives home the certainty and the searching nature of the division: in bed or at the mill, at rest or at work, among men or among women, the coming will divide, taking the one and leaving the other. The verse thus reinforces the lesson of the discriminating judgment, extending it to the humblest sphere of daily life, that every soul, of whatever station, may know it must be found among the taken, prepared and believing, when the day comes.
36:
"Two men shall be in the field; the one shall be taken, and the other left." The witnesses note the third example (omitted in some manuscripts) of the discriminating judgment. Gill describes the scene: "two men shall be in the field, at work there, tilling the ground, or sowing the seed, or reaping the corn" — companions in labour, yet divided at the coming. Cyril of Jerusalem continues his pastoral comfort to every station: "Though thou be in the field, the Angels shall take thee; think not that He will take the landowners and leave thee the husbandman... Though thou be a slave, though thou be poor, be not distressed; He who took the form of a servant despises not servants."

The witnesses note the third pair, men in the field, once more divided — companions in toil, yet one taken and one left.

So the witnesses make the verse the third instance of the great division. The lesson the expositors draw is the same searching truth, now set among the labourers of the field: that the coming divides those joined in common toil, according to faith and not station. The witnesses note the figure: two men working together in the field — ploughing, sowing, or reaping — companions in the same labour, side by side; yet "the one shall be taken, and the other left." The expositors press the cumulative force of the three examples (the bed, the mill, the field): in every sphere of life — rest and labour, men and women, household and farm — the coming will sunder those most closely joined, by the hidden difference of faith. Cyril of Jerusalem's comfort, the witnesses observe, reaches here its fullest expression: the poor husbandman in the field, the slave, the lowly labourer, need not fear that the Judge will pass over them in favour of the great; for the angels will take the faithful labourer as readily as the landowner — indeed, "He who took the form of a servant despises not servants." The expositors note the levelling truth: the judgment is no respecter of rank or wealth; the field-labourer, the slave, the poor, if found in faith, will be taken, while the great, if unbelieving, will be left. The witnesses draw the lesson the three figures together teach: that the coming of the Son of man will be a universal and searching division, reaching into every station and occupation, separating the believing from the unbelieving regardless of outward condition; and that no man's lowliness need exclude him, nor any man's greatness secure him, for the division runs by the heart. The verse thus completes the triple picture of the discriminating judgment — bed, mill, and field — pressing upon every reader, of whatever station, the urgency of being found among the taken; and it prepares for the disciples' question, "Where, Lord?", which the final verse will answer.
37:
"And they answered and said unto him, Where, Lord? And he said unto them, Wheresoever the body is, thither will the eagles be gathered together." The witnesses note the disciples' question and Christ's proverbial answer. Gill explains the question: the disciples, hearing of the division, ask "where" — "not where the persons would be left, but whither the others would be taken." Barnes reads the answer as pointing to the place of judgment: "where there is the most guilt and wickedness; eagles flock where there is prey; so these armies will flock to the place where there is the most wickedness" — directing their thoughts to Jerusalem, "the place of eminent wickedness," where the calamities would begin (Job 39:30; Matthew 24:28). Clarke gives the figure: "where the dead carcass is, there will the birds of prey be."

The witnesses note Christ answers the "where?" with a proverb: as eagles or vultures gather to a carcass, so judgment will gather where the corruption is.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the discourse with its enigmatic but pointed proverb. The lesson the expositors draw is that judgment falls where guilt and corruption are, gathering inevitably to the place ripe for it. The disciples, the witnesses note, ask "Where, Lord?" — wondering, as Gill suggests, not where the rejected would be left, but whither the taken would be gathered, or where these great events would fall. Christ answers, the expositors press, not with a place-name but with a proverb: "Wheresoever the body is, thither will the eagles be gathered together" — as birds of prey, eagles or vultures, gather unerringly to a carcass, drawn by the corruption, so the instruments of judgment gather to the place ripe for destruction. Barnes draws the primary application, the witnesses observe: the figure pointed the disciples to Jerusalem — the body, the carcass, the place of eminent guilt and spiritual death — to which the Roman armies (the eagles, the very standard of Rome) would flock as vultures to a corpse, for judgment gathers where wickedness is greatest. The expositors note the deeper principle the proverb carries: judgment is not arbitrary but moral; it falls where it is deserved, gathering to corruption as surely as birds of prey to a carcass; the morally dead draw the executioners of divine wrath. The witnesses observe the double reference once more: the proverb suited the gathering of the Roman eagles to doomed Jerusalem, and points also to the final judgment, which will gather to wherever spiritual death and guilt are found. The expositors draw the solemn lesson with which the discourse ends: men need not ask curiously "where" the judgment will fall, for it falls where it must — upon the corrupt and the guilty, drawn to them as vultures to the slain. The verse thus closes the teaching on the coming of the Son of man with a warning as searching as it is brief: that judgment seeks out corruption infallibly, and that the soul or the city ripe for it will not escape the gathering of the eagles.
